labels.name: Class
labels.desc: A blueprint that defines structure and behavior

labels.name: Object
labels.desc: An instance created from a class

labels.name: Method
labels.desc: A behavior defined on a class

labels.name: Field
labels.desc: A piece of state stored on an object
labels.example:
private boolean occupied;
private boolean cleaned;

labels.name: Getter
labels.desc: A method that reads or computes a value — may derive from other fields rather than storing it directly

labels.name: Static
labels.desc: Belongs to the class itself, not any single instance

labels.name: Condition
labels.desc: A decision or branch based on a value

labels.name: Encapsulation
labels.desc: Keeping fields private, exposing them only through controlled methods

prompts.title: Room Availability
prompts.statement: A hotel management system tracks whether each room is occupied and whether it has been cleaned. A room should only appear as available when both conditions are met — it's clean and no one is in it.

expectedTags.phrase: tracks whether each room is occupied and whether it has been cleaned
expectedTags.explanation: Whether a room is occupied and whether it is cleaned are two separate pieces of state that each room must remember — these are fields. Keeping them internal to the room and exposing them only through controlled access is encapsulation.

codeSnippets.field:
private boolean occupied;
private boolean cleaned;

expectedTags.phrase: only appear as available when both conditions are met
expectedTags.explanation: Availability is derived by checking two conditions at once rather than being stored directly — making this a computed getter. The requirement that both must be true is a logical condition the code must evaluate.

expectedTags.phrase: it's clean and no one is in it
expectedTags.explanation: This phrase spells out the two-part rule that drives the availability check — the method must branch on both of these conditions to return the correct result.

prompts.title: Reservation Pricing
prompts.statement: A reservation system calculates the price of a stay based on room type and dates. King rooms cost more per night than doubles, and weekend nights carry a higher rate. The booking should be able to report its total cost at any time.

expectedTags.phrase: room type and dates
expectedTags.explanation: Room type and date information are the pieces of state a reservation must remember — these become the private fields that the rest of the pricing logic reads from.

expectedTags.phrase: report its total cost at any time
expectedTags.explanation: Total cost is not stored directly on the reservation — it is computed on demand from the other fields each time it is requested, which is the defining trait of a computed getter.

expectedTags.phrase: King rooms cost more per night than doubles
expectedTags.explanation: Choosing a different rate based on room type requires a branch in the logic — the code must check which room type applies before it can set the correct nightly rate.

expectedTags.phrase: weekend nights carry a higher rate
expectedTags.explanation: Applying a weekend surcharge only when the stay includes a weekend is a conditional step — the code checks a flag and adjusts the rate only if that condition is true.

prompts.title: Employee Pay Calculation
prompts.statement: A payroll system tracks each employee's hourly rate and hours worked for the week. The system needs to report regular hours, overtime hours, and total pay — but those values should be derived on demand, not stored separately. Up to 40 hours in the week counts as regular time; hours beyond that are overtime.

expectedTags.phrase: hourly rate and hours worked for the week
expectedTags.explanation: Pay rate and hours worked are the core facts the system must remember about each employee — they become private fields whose values drive all downstream calculations.

codeSnippets.field:
private double payRate;
private double hoursWorked;
codeSnippets.encapsulation:
private double payRate;
private double hoursWorked;

expectedTags.phrase: report regular hours, overtime hours, and total pay
expectedTags.explanation: Each of these three values is computed from the stored fields rather than looked up from a stored copy — making them computed getters that calculate and return a result on demand.

expectedTags.phrase: derived on demand, not stored separately
expectedTags.explanation: Keeping derived values out of storage and computing them through methods is a form of encapsulation — it ensures that the internal fields are the single source of truth and outside code cannot accidentally corrupt a cached value.

expectedTags.phrase: Up to 40 hours in the week counts as regular time
expectedTags.explanation: Regular hours are capped at 40 — determining how many hours count as regular requires a condition that checks whether total hours exceed that threshold.

expectedTags.phrase: hours beyond that are overtime
expectedTags.explanation: Overtime only exists when hours exceed 40, so computing it requires a condition that returns zero when the employee has not crossed that boundary.

prompts.title: Hotel Booking
prompts.statement: A hotel tracks its total suites and standard rooms and how many of each are currently reserved. Only the hotel's own booking process should be able to change reservation counts — outside systems should only be able to read availability. The system needs to accept booking requests and confirm whether they succeeded.

expectedTags.phrase: total suites and standard rooms and how many of each are currently reserved
expectedTags.explanation: The total capacity and current reservation counts for each room type are pieces of state the hotel must store — these become private fields so that only the hotel's own logic can modify them.

expectedTags.phrase: outside systems should only be able to read availability
expectedTags.explanation: Availability is computed from the stored counts and exposed as a read-only value — this is a computed getter, and restricting outside access to reads only is encapsulation in action.

expectedTags.phrase: accept booking requests and confirm whether they succeeded
expectedTags.explanation: Processing a booking is a behavior the hotel exposes through a method. Whether a booking can proceed depends on a condition — checking that enough rooms are currently available before making any changes.

prompts.title: Name Formatter
prompts.statement: An application needs to format names into a standard display pattern like 'Johnson, Dr. Mel B, PhD'. Callers should not need to create anything to use this — it should work like a utility available directly.

expectedTags.phrase: format names into a standard display pattern
expectedTags.explanation: Formatting a name is a behavior — it takes inputs, applies a rule, and returns a result. That makes it a method, and offering multiple variations (with or without title, with or without suffix) means multiple method signatures.

expectedTags.phrase: Callers should not need to create anything to use this
expectedTags.explanation: When a utility works without requiring the caller to create an instance first, its methods belong to the type itself rather than to any particular object — that is what static means, and it means no object needs to be created.

codeSnippets.static: public static String format(String lastName, String firstName) { ... }
codeSnippets.object:
// No object needed:
String formatted = NameFormatter.format("Johnson", "Mel");

expectedTags.phrase: work like a utility available directly
expectedTags.explanation: Being callable directly — without going through an instance — is the defining characteristic of a static method. The caller references the type name itself rather than any created object.

prompts.title: Dealership Architecture
prompts.statement: A vehicle dealership application has grown too large to manage as a single unit. It needs to be split into focused pieces: one holds vehicle data, one manages the inventory, one handles file reads and writes, one handles all user interaction, and one whose only job is to start the program.

expectedTags.phrase: one holds vehicle data
expectedTags.explanation: The piece responsible for vehicle data stores the individual attributes of each vehicle as fields and exposes them through getters so other parts of the application can read them.

expectedTags.phrase: one manages the inventory
expectedTags.explanation: Managing inventory means working with a collection of vehicle objects — this piece defines its own blueprint (class) and operates on the vehicle objects the data piece describes.

expectedTags.phrase: one handles file reads and writes
expectedTags.explanation: File I/O is a focused responsibility — it belongs in its own blueprint (class) and is exposed through methods that other parts of the application call to load or save data.

codeSnippets.method:
public List<Vehicle> getVehicles() { /* reads from file */ }
public void saveVehicles(List<Vehicle> vehicles) { /* writes to file */ }

expectedTags.phrase: one handles all user interaction
expectedTags.explanation: The piece dedicated to user interaction is its own blueprint — it defines the menus, input prompts, and display logic as methods that respond to the user.

codeSnippets.class: public class UserInterface { /* Scanner, menus, display */ }
codeSnippets.method:
public void run() { /* main interaction loop */ }
public void displayMenu() { /* show options */ }

expectedTags.phrase: one whose only job is to start the program
expectedTags.explanation: The entry point of a Java program is the main method, which must be static so the runtime can call it without creating an instance first. A class whose only job is to start the program exists solely to host that static method.

prompts.title: Bank Account
prompts.statement: A bank account needs to protect its balance. No code outside the account should be able to set the balance directly. The only ways to change it should be deposits and withdrawals — and a withdrawal should be rejected if there aren't enough funds.

expectedTags.phrase: protect its balance
expectedTags.explanation: Protecting the balance means keeping it hidden from direct outside access — this is the core idea of encapsulation, where internal state is shielded and only reachable through controlled entry points.

expectedTags.phrase: No code outside the account should be able to set the balance directly
expectedTags.explanation: Making the balance a private field and providing no public setter enforces encapsulation — outside code cannot reach in and change the value without going through the account's own logic.

codeSnippets.encapsulation: private double balance; // no public setter

expectedTags.phrase: The only ways to change it should be deposits and withdrawals
expectedTags.explanation: Deposits and withdrawals are behaviors — they are methods that the account exposes as the controlled pathways for changing the balance. Allowing change only through these methods is encapsulation.

codeSnippets.encapsulation:
// Only deposit() and withdraw() can change the balance
private double balance;

expectedTags.phrase: a withdrawal should be rejected if there aren't enough funds
expectedTags.explanation: Before completing a withdrawal the code must check whether the requested amount is available — this check is a condition that determines whether the balance changes or the request is turned away.

prompts.title: Card Game
prompts.statement: A simple card game deals two cards to each player from a shuffled deck. After dealing, each player's hand has a total point value, and the highest total wins.

expectedTags.phrase: a shuffled deck
expectedTags.explanation: A deck is a concrete thing the program creates and works with — it is an object, an instance brought to life from a blueprint that knows how to hold and shuffle cards.

expectedTags.phrase: each player
expectedTags.explanation: Each player is also a distinct object — a separate instance that holds its own hand and accumulates its own cards independently of the other player.

codeSnippets.object:
Player player1 = new Player("Alice");
Player player2 = new Player("Bob");

expectedTags.phrase: deals two cards to each player
expectedTags.explanation: Dealing is a behavior the deck performs — it has a defined process for removing a card from the top and delivering it. Calling that behavior on the deck produces the cards each player's hand receives.

expectedTags.phrase: each player's hand has a total point value
expectedTags.explanation: The total point value is derived by summing the cards in the hand rather than being stored as a fixed number — retrieving it is a computed getter that calculates the answer from the hand's current contents.

codeSnippets.getter:
int score1 = player1.getHand().getTotalValue();
int score2 = player2.getHand().getTotalValue();

expectedTags.phrase: the highest total wins
expectedTags.explanation: Determining who wins requires comparing the two scores — the code branches based on which total is greater, which is a condition that drives the final output.
